content.full_text:
Memorandum

To: Alice  
Date: Jan. 9, 1981

From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION  
Sarah Cameron

Subject: Computing the reporting threshold for income to a business entity when the filer and the filer's spouse both have a share of the entity.

Under the definition of "income" in Section 82030, an individual's income includes a pro rata share of any income of a business entity in which the individual or spouse has a 10 percent or greater interest. In addition, a filer's income includes a community property interest in the income of a spouse. Since a community property interest is 50%, a filer has a 50% interest in the spouse's pro rata share of income to a business entity, in addition to the filer's own pro rata share. Thus, if a filer has a 10% share and his or her spouse also has a 10% share, the filer is attributed with a 15% share for purposes of determining which sources of income to the business entity are reportable. This is because half of the spouse share, i.e., 5% is added to the filer's 10% share.

Sources of income to a business entity are reportable at a $10,000 pro rata level. Thus in the situation described above, the filer would have to report anyone who paid roughly $66,666.66 in gross receipts to the business entity, because 15% of $66,666.66 is $9999.99. So 15% of slightly more than that amount would be $10,000. Thus, if the filer and the filer's spouse have shares in a business entity, the threshold remains the same, $10,000, but the filer's pro rata share increases for determining that the threshold has been met. You should not think of the threshold as being lowered in such a situation; you should focus on what the pro rata share attributable to the filer is.

A good example of why you should not try to think in terms of lowering the threshold is the following: the filer owns 10% and the filer's spouse owns 50%. In this situation, it's not correct to think of the threshold as being lowered to $6,666.66. That only works when the shares of the filer and the spouse are equal. In this case, the filer is attributed with his or her own 10% share and half of the spouse's share, i.e., 25%. So the filer must report if a 35% share of the gross receipts of a client or customer is $10,000. As I figure it out, this means someone who has paid the business $28,571.42. (This may be off as I'm not that good at long division.) If you wanted to lower the threshold and apply only his 10% figure, you would have to lower it to $2,857.14.
